note: Predictions computed and recorded BEFORE the corresponding Bosch ground truth arrived. Ceiling anchor 8.99 ft (laser). Two independent methods: (a) video = consensus min-area rects of per-frame HorizonNet layouts from the June flythrough (44 frames, layout/apartment-1112/rooms.json); (b) pano = HorizonNet on the single named 360 still per room (still_layout, projects/apartment-1112/still/).

master_bedroom.scored: video L -2.9%, pano S +8.7%; BOTH methods over-span the short axis (door-through into the small hall, the suspected mechanism)

guest_bedroom_aka_bonus.scored: pano L +3.0%, S +1.7% - the one-pano-per-room protocol's flagship result

secondary_bath_jack_and_jill.scored: VIDEO wins: +5.6% / +5.2%. Pano L +3.3% but width +46.7% — over-spanned through the jack-and-jill doors AND the shower curtain (HorizonNet cannot see the wall behind a curtain)

primary_bath.scored: SPLIT: pano L +1.1%, video S +5.0%. Pano width +37% — the shower nook + closet doorway. Ensemble rule emerges: pano for lengths, video-consensus for widths in small rooms with openings

great_room.pano_ft: kitchen still 32.8 x 15.0, living still 31.6 x 13.4
great_room.scored_diagonal: kitchen-pano rect implies diag 36.09 (+2.5%), living-pano 34.30 (-2.6%) — the two bracket truth, ensemble mean 35.19 = -0.05%. Video rect implies 38.08 (+8.1%), its 19 ft width over-span showing. Wall-parallel L and W still open; diagonal constrains W = sqrt(35.21^2 - L^2), so one wall measurement closes the room

small_hall.scored: width +6.2% (the corridor-sliver frames were right); length wildly over — lengthwise corridor views span through doorways into connected halls, as expected
